# MAIN ARTICLE

# Materials Science & Engineering C


journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/msec


# Co3O4/CuO hollow nanocage hybrids with high oxidase-like activity for biosensing of dopamine


Yunxia Zhuang a , Xiaodan Zhang a , Qiumeng Chen a , Siqi Li a , Haiyan Cao b, ⁎ , Yuming Huang a, ⁎

- a The Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-time Analytical Chemistry, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University,

Chongqing400715, China

b The Key Laboratory of Chongqing Inorganic Special Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangtze Normal University, Chongqing408100, China


# A R T I C L E I N F O


Keywords: Co3O4/CuO nanocomposites Hollow nanocage Oxidase mimetic Dopamine Sensor


# 1. Introduction


Since thefirst work on the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic nanoparticles (NPs) in2007 [1], nanomaterial-based enzyme mimics (nanozymes) have continued to attract interest for applications in variousfields, including bioanalysis, environmental analysis, and medical treatment [2 -5]. This is because they can overcome some of the shortcomings of natural enzymes, such as the high cost of preparation and purification, and difficulties in recovery and recycling. Various nanozymes have been developed, including carbonbased nanomaterials [6,7], selenide and sulfide nanomaterials [8 -10], metal nanomaterials [11,12], and metal oxide nanomaterials [13 -16]. However, the current research in thefield of nanozymes is mostly concentrated on peroxidase mimics, and little attention is being paid to oxidase mimics [3]. It is well known that H2O2is needed as an electron acceptor for peroxidase-like catalyzed reactions. However, because of its strong oxidizing property, H2O2can destroy some targets [17]. Furthermore, H2O2readily decomposes and loses its oxidation capacity. Unlike peroxidase mimics, oxidase mimics can catalyze oxidation of

⁎

Corresponding authors. E-mail addresses:


# A B S T R A C T


Here, we report the synthesis, characterization, and oxidase-like activity of Co3O4/CuO hollow nanocage (HNC) nanocomposites. The Co3O4/CuO HNCs were successfully prepared by hydrothermal treatment of ZIF-67in copper nitrate solution, followed by carbonization in air. The obtained nanocomposites were characterized by scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction. The Co3O4/CuO HNCs possess enhanced oxidase-like activity compared with pure Co3O4and CuO, and they can catalytically oxidize the3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate in the absence of H2O2to produce an intense blue product. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) measurements indicate that1O2and O2 % -radicals are the major ROS in catalytic oxidation of TMB by dissolved oxygen in the TMB -Co3O4/CuO HNC system. We then developed a simple, sensitive, visual, and colorimetric biosensing method for dopamine (DA) based on its inhibiting effect on TMB oxidation. The proposed method allows for DA detection with a limit of detection of0.027 μ Mand a dynamic range of0.05 -8 μ M. This new colorimetric method was successfully used to detect DA in biological samples. The present work demonstrates a simple strategy to fabricate an efficient oxidase mimic with potential applications in bioanalysis and clinical diagnosis.

some organic substrates without H2O2. This unique advantage makes oxidase mimics an appealing alternative for constructing sensors with simple operation, good compatibility, and high reliability.

The superior catalytic properties of noble-metal-based nanostructures make them promising oxidase mimic candidates for applications in biocatalysis. For example, some noble metal NPs have been shown to exhibit oxidase-like activity, including Au NPs [18], Ag NPs [19], Pt NPs [20], and the noble metal alloys Au@Pt NPs [21], Au@Ag NPs [22], and Au@PdPt NPs [23]. Although noble metals offer superior catalytic activity, they are expensive and a limited resource, which inevitably hinders their wide application. Therefore, low cost and available non-noble metal oxides, particularly transition-metal oxides with comparable activities, are good candidates for oxidase mimics [13,24 -28].

Metal -organic frameworks (MOFs), a unique type of emerging material, have received considerable attention in variousfields, such as gas storage [29], catalysis [30], energy storage systems [31], and electrochemical sensors [32]. They also can act as self-sacrificial templates to synthesize hollow and porous metal oxides, which possess caohaiyan@yznu.edu.cn (H. Cao), ymhuang@swu.edu.cn (Y. Huang).

innumerable reaction sites, high porosity, and easy gas accessibility [33]. Among these metal oxides, Co3O4nanostructures derived from MOFs have received great attention because of their excellent catalytic activity, ease of preparation, good response stability, and low cost [34]. For instance, hollow Co3O4derived from ZIF-67exhibits high catalytic activity and stability for CO oxidation [35], it can be used as the anode material for Li-ion battery [36], and it is an excellent supercapacitor electrode material [37]. Mu et al. [38] showed that Co3O4NPs with different morphologies exhibit varied catalytic activities as catalase mimics. Ding et al. [39] reported that perylene-diimide-modified Co3O4NPs exhibit enhanced peroxidase-like activity. To further improve the catalytic activity, hybrid nanocomposites have been designed, and they exhibit superior catalytic performance to their monometallic counterparts because of the electronic structure effect and synergistic effect [40]. For example, Koo and co-workers [33] found that PdO-functionalized Co3O4hollow nanocages (HNCs) exhibit a higher response to acetone than single Co3O4nanomaterials. Zhu et al. [41] reported that MOF-derived ZnO/Co3O4nanocomposites show enhanced electrochemical performance compared with the single Co3O4and ZnO NPs. Li et al. [42] synthesized double-shelled Fe2O3/Co3O4hollow microcubes with improved electrochemical performance owing to their unique microstructure and the synergistic effect between Fe2O3and Co3O4. These examples demonstrate that constructing metal oxide nanocomposites is an effective way to obtain high-performance catalysts.

In this study, based on the excellent properties of Co3O4HNCs, CuO NPs and Co3O4HNCs were integrated into nanocomposites by hydrothermal treatment of ZIF-67in Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, followed by carbonization in air. CuO is selected because it is a typical p-type semiconductor and a promising catalyst with good catalytic activity in sensor development [43], degradation of organic pollutants [44], and so forth. The as-prepared Co3O4/CuO HNCs were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). For comparison, single Co3O4HNCs and CuO NPs were also prepared, and their structural characteristics and oxidase-like activities were evaluated. The reaction kinetics and mechanism were investigated. Finally, a new and facile colorimetric method was developed with the Co3O4/CuO HNC composite as an oxidase mimic for detection of dopamine (DA) in human serum and DA hydrochloride injection. This is thefirst report of using MOFs as starting template materials to produce bimetallic oxide nanostructures with excellent oxidase mimic activity for sensing DA.


# 2. Experimental section



# 2.1. Chemicals


All of the chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used as received without any further purification. Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O) and copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O) were purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). 2-Methylimidazole (MIM), DA hydrochloride (DA), and p -benzoquinone were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). 3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and superoxide dismutase (SOD) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate (NaAc), ethanol, and methanol were supplied by Chongqing Taixin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chongqing, China). Glucose and lactose were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glycine was purchased from Chengdu Chemical Reagents Company (Sichuan, China).


# 2.2. Instrumentation


The SEM images were recorded with a Hitachi model S-4800fi eld emission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of25kV. The XRD patterns of the as-prepared products were recorded with a TD-3500X-ray diffractometer (Dandong, China) under the conditions of nickelfi ltered Cu K α radiation ( λ =0.15406nm), a current of20mA, a voltage of30kV, and a scanning rate of4° min -1in the angle range5 -80° (2 θ ). A transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2F20) equipped with an energy dispersed X-ray detector (EDX) operating at an accelerating voltage of200kV was used for detailed microstructure and composition analysis of the prepared samples. The XPS and X-ray excited auger electron spectroscopy (XE-AES) measurements were performed with a Thermo ESCALAB250XI multifunctional imaging electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.) equipped with an Al K α radiation source (1486.6eV). The Co3O4/CuO HNC samples for the XPS and XE-AES measurements were prepared in a spherical shape with a diameter of about500 μ m. The working pressure was8 × 10 -10Pa. The Co2p, Cu2p, O1s, C1s, Cu LMM, and Co LMM spectra were recorded under the following conditions: pass energy30eV, 0.1eV step -1 , working voltage12.5kV, andfilament current16mA. All of the binding energies were calibrated by referencing to the C1s binding energy (285.0eV). The adsorption measurements were performed with a UV-2450spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Suzhou).


# 2.3. Synthesis of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs


First, ZIF-67was synthesized according to a previously reported method [45]. In brief, 498mg of cobalt nitrate hexahydrate was dissolved in50mL of methanol, and656mg of2-methylimidazole was dissolved in50mL methanol. The latter solution was poured into the former pink solution with vigorous stirring for30min. The mixture was then incubated at room temperature without stirring. After24h, a dark purple solid was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with methanol, and the obtained ZIF-67was then dried at60 °C overnight. To prepare the Co3O4/CuO HNCs, 80mg of as-prepared ZIF-67was dissolved in50mL of ethanol. After ultrasonic treatment of the solution for1min, 40mg of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O was added and the mixture was then heated to90 °C and kept at this temperature for3h. The product was collected by centrifugation and dried at60 °C to obtain ZIF-67/Cu hydroxide (HD), which was carbonized at360 °C for2h in air to obtain Co3O4/CuO HNCs. For comparison, we also synthesized Co3O4and CuO by direct carbonation of ZIF-67in a muffle furnace at360 °C for2h in air and a rapid-precipitation method, respectively (see the Supporting information).


# 2.4. Catalytic activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs and kinetic analysis


The catalytic activity of the prepared Co3O4/CuO HNCs was evaluated based on the TMB color reaction. In brief, 0.5mL of200mg/L Co3O4/CuO HNCs and0.25mL of2mM TMB were added to4.25mL of0.2M NaAc -HAc buffer (pH = 4.0). The mixture was incubated at40 °C for20min, and then used for ultraviolet -visible absorption spectroscopy measurements.

The kinetic measurements were performed in time course mode by monitoring the absorbance change at652nm in1min intervals. The Michaelis -Menten constant was calculated using Lineweaver -Burk plots of the double reciprocal of the Michaelis-Menten equation: 1/ ν = Km/ Vmax ⋅ (1/[S] + 1/ Km), where v is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, and [S] is the concentration of the substrate. Km is the Michaelis -Menten constant, which indicates the enzyme affi nity for the substrate.


# 2.5. Application of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs as oxidase mimetics for DA detection


For DA analysis, the calibration curve for DA wasfirstly obtained. The absorption spectra of mixtures containing Co3O4/CuO HNCs (0.5mL, 200mg/L), 0.25mL DA with various concentrations from0to2mM, TMB (0.25mL, 2mM), and NaAc-HAc buffer (4.0mL, 0.2M, pH4.0) were recorded from500to800nm after20min reaction at

40 °C. The net absorbance at652nm ( Δ A = A0 -A , where A0is the absorbance of the blank solution and A is the absorbance of the DA sample solution) versus the DA concentration was then used to construct the calibration curve. For DA determination in serum samples, the human serum samples were centrifuged by ultrafiltration with a30kDa Amicon cell for30min at3000rpm, diluted100-fold, and then mixed with Co3O4/CuO HNCs, TMB, and NaAc-HAc buffer, as for the DA standard. After20min incubation at40 °C, the colorimetric measurement was performed. The DA hydrochloride injection was subjected to proper dilution and then used for DA determination. The DA hydrochloride injection and serum samples were obtained from a local hospital (The Ninth Hospital of Chongqing, Beibei).


# 3. Results and discussion



# 3.1. Characterization of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs


SEM confirmed that the ZIF-67particles are rhombic dodecahedron shaped (Fig. S1a), which is consistent with a previous study [36]. The obtained ZIF-67polyhedron structures have a smooth surface with an average particle size of991nm based on the statistics of67particles (Fig. S2a). ZIF-67/Cu HD nanostructures formed by hydrothermal treatment of ZIF-67in an aqueous solution of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O. During this process, hydrolysis of Cu2+ ions produces protons, which gradually etch the ZIF-67template to form ZIF-67/Cu HD with reduction of the particle size to730nm based on the statistics of70particles (Figs. S1b and S2b). Co3O4/CuO HNC structures were obtained by carbonization of ZIF-67/Cu HD. As shown in Fig. 1a and b, the obtained Co3O4/CuO HNCs retain the polyhedral shape but have a rougher surface than ZIF67. The average size of the obtained Co3O4/CuO HNCs is607nm based on the statistics of67particles (Figs. S1c and S2c), showing a further decrease in the size compared with that of ZIF-67/Cu HD. This is probably because of dimensional shrinkage and cleavage of the organic ligands of ZIF-67in the carbonization process. From the enlarged image in Fig. 1a, there is an obvious cavity from broken Co3O4/CuO particles (Fig. 1b), indicating formation of a hollow structure as well as a relatively rough surface. Moreover, the TEM images in Fig. 1c and d show the hollow structure of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs. The rough surfaces are mainly caused by formation of a Co3O4/CuO nanoparticle assembly. The unambiguous interplanar distances of0.20, 0.25, and0.23nm in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs (Fig. 1e) can be indexed to the (400) planes of Co3O4 [46], (311) planes of Co3O4 [46], and (111) planes of CuO [47], respectively.

EDX analysis indicates that the Co3O4/CuO HNCs have high carbon content of74.6% (Fig. S3a). The elemental ratio of Co to Cu is3:2, which is lower than that of the initial incorporation ratio of2:1. This possible because during the process of hydrothermal treatment of ZIF67in an aqueous solution of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, ZIF-67is gradually etched by the protons released from the hydrolysis of copper ions in solution, leading to leaching of Co2+ ions. Interestingly, the absence of nitrogen indicates that the ligands of ZIF-67are completely decomposed (Fig. S3a), and thus ZIF-67/Cu HD is completely converted to Co3O4/CuO HNCs. Furthermore, the selected-area electron diffraction image of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs shows concentric rings (Fig. 1f), indicating various crystalline planes of the as-prepared Co3O4/CuO HNCs.

The XRD patterns of ZIF-67, ZIF-67/Cu HD, Co3O4, and the Co3O4/ CuO HNCs are shown in Fig. 2a and Fig. S3b. The diffraction peaks of as-prepared ZIF-67and ZIF-67/Cu HD are almost identical to that of simulated ZIF-67 (Fig. S3b). When the as-formed ZIF-67/Cu HD powder was annealed at360 °C for2h in air, the XRD pattern clearly changes with the appearance of six new diffraction peaks at2 θ =19.2°, 31.5°, 36.8°, 44.8°, 55.7°, 59.4°, and65.2°, corresponding to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) crystal planes of cubic phase Co3O4, respectively [46]. Compared with the diffraction peaks of Co3O4, it is clear that the diffraction peaks of Co3O4/CuO HNCs are broader. This is attributed to introduction of CuO into Co3O4, because the diffraction peaks at2 θ =35.3° and38.2° correspond to the (111) planes of CuO (PDF #65-2309). To analyze the chemical composition of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs, we performed XPS analysis. As shown in the XPS full survey spectrum (Fig. S3c), the as-synthesized Co3O4/CuO HNCs contain Co, Cu, C, and O elements, which is consistent with the EDX analysis. The high-resolution spectrum of Co2p exhibits two characteristics bands at779.2and794.2eV, corresponding to Co2p3/2and Co2p1/2 [48], respectively. The two small peaks at788.2and805.2eV are typical Co shake-up satellite peaks (denoted as ' sat. ' ). As shown in Fig. 2b, thefitting peaks located at781.0and779.6eV correspond to Co2+ 2p3/2and Co3+ 2p1/2, respectively. The Co2p1/2peak can be divided into two peaks at794.6and796.3eV, indicating coexistence of Co(II) and Co(III) and confirming successful formation of Co3O4 [49,50]. Based on the XE-AES spectrum of Co LMM (Fig. S3d), the Auger parameter α ' is estimated to be1553.3eV (Table S1), which is in agreement with that previously reported for Co3O4 [51]. The Cu2p spectrum in Fig. 2c shows one major Cu2p1/2peak at953.7eV with a satellite peak at962.2eV, and a major Cu2p3/2peak at934.0eV with two satellites peaks at941.3and943.7eV. This indicates existence of Cu2+ from the CuO phase [52 -54]. Based on the XE-AES spectrum of Cu LMM (Fig. S3e), the Auger parameter α ′ is estimated to be1851.5eV (Table S1), which is in agreement with that previously reported for CuO [55]. The O1s peak at531.2eV is attributed to -OH of the defect-oxide or hydroxyl group and dissociated oxygen [48]. The O1s peak at529.3eV corresponds to the metal -oxygen bonds of both Co3O4 [48] and CuO [48,52,56]. Nitrogen adsorption -desorption measurements of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs were performed to obtain the pore size distribution. As shown in Fig. S4a, the carbonized samples exhibit type IV isotherm characteristics, indicating existence of mesoporous structures. In addition, the pore size of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs is larger than that of Co3O4 (Fig. S4b), which means that there are more active sites for the catalytic reaction.


# 3.2. Oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs


The oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs was evaluated by catalytic oxidation of TMB in the absence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 3a, direct oxidation of TMB by dissolved oxygen in the absence or presence of CuO produces negligible absorbance. TMB oxidation by dissolved oxygen in the presence of Co3O4gives a light-blue color reaction. However, the as-synthesized Co3O4/CuO HNCs can catalyze oxidation of colorless TMB by dissolved oxygen to produce a blue product (oxTMB) with an obvious absorption peak at652nm (Fig. 3a). The absorbance of the TMB/Co3O4/CuO HNC system at652nm is much higher than that of the TMB/CuO system and TMB/Co3O4system, revealing that the as-prepared Co3O4/CuO HNCs exhibit enhanced oxidase-like catalytic activity under the same reaction conditions. Furthermore, the oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNC nanocomposite is superior to the sum of activities of the Co3O4HNCs and CuO NPs, indicating a synergistic effect between the Co3O4HNCs and CuO NPs. The synergistic effect between Co3O4and CuO has been reported in previous studies [57 -60]. For example, Zhang and coworkers [57] found that hierarchical Co3O4/CuO nanowire exhibits a potential synergistic effect with remarkably enhanced electrochemical performance. Gao and co-workers [58] synthesized Cu-modified cobalt oxides with hollow polyhedral nanocages, and they found that they are efficient electrocatalytic and photocatalytic water oxidation catalysts. Chen and co-workers [59] investigated synergistic degradation of phenols with the CuO -Co3O4@MnO2nanocatalyst. Yin and co-workers [60] reported synergistic degradation of phenols by bimetallic CuO -Co3O4supported on the γ -Al2O3catalyst in the H2O2/HCO3 -system. Unfortunately, the mechanism for the observed synergistic effect is still unclear [59,60]. Based on the studies of Gao and co-workers [58] and Chen and co-workers [59], we suggest a possible reason for the observed synergistic effect between the Co3O4HNCs and CuO NPs. In the Co3O4/CuO HNCs, introduction of electrophilic Cu2+ into Co3O4

Fig. 1. (a) SEM image of Co3O4/CuO HNCs. (b) The enlarged SEM image of Co3O4/CuO HNCs in the red circle in Fig. 1(a). (c, d) TEM images of Co3O4/CuO HNCs. (e) HRTEM image of Co3O4/CuO HNCs. (f) SAED patterns of Co3O4/CuO HNCs. (For interpretation of the references to color in thisfigure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

makes the adjacent Co more electron withdrawing [58], facilitating the electron transfer ability andfinally improving the oxidase-like catalytic activity. In addition, the mesoporous hollow structure of the Co3O4/ CuO catalyst (Fig. S4b) is favorable for exposing active sites to the substrate and increasing the number of catalytic active sites [59], leading to significant enhancement of the catalytic activity. Therefore, the synergistic effect of the higher electron-withdrawing ability of Co in the Co3O4/CuO HNCs owing to incorporation of electrophilic Cu2+ and the hollow microstructures of Co3O4/CuO effectively promoting charge transport and exposing catalytic active sites may result in significantly enhanced oxidase-like activity.

The origin of the oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs was investigated. First, N2- and O2-purging experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. S5a, the catalytic activity of Co3O4/CuO HNCs decreases after the solution is purged with N2, while it increases after bubbling pure O2into the solution. These results indicate the important role of dissolved oxygen in the reaction system and further verify the effective oxidase-like activity of Co3O4/CuO HNCs. Second, a series of experiments were performed to investigate the ROS in this system, and the results are shown in Fig. S5. Addition of p -benzoquinone (scavenger of the superoxide radical (O2 · -) [61]) and NaN3 (scavenger of singlet molecular oxygen ( 1O2) [62]) decreases the catalytic activity compared with the control experiments, while the presence of tert -butanol (scavenger of the hydroxyl radical (·OH) [63]) has a minor influence. The results indicate generation of1O2and O2 % -radicals in the catalytic process. Furthermore, the catalytic activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs clearly changes in the presence of superoxide dismutase (SOD, specific O2 % -scavenger) (Fig. S5a), confirming the important role of the O2 % -radical in the TMB-oxidation reaction. In addition, β -carotene has a significant influence on the oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs (Fig. S5b), further confirming generation of1O2during the catalytic reaction [64].

Based on above results, it can be deduced that the oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs originates from their catalytic ability for activation of dissolved oxygen to generate1O2and O2 % -in the TMB oxidation reaction. It has been speculated that porous Co3O4/CuO HNCs facilitate rapid diffusion of dissolved oxygen to the catalytic active sites [65,66]. In addition, enhancement of the catalytic activity can

Fig. 2. (a) XRD patterns of Co3O4and Co3O4/CuO HNCs. (b -d) Co2p, Cu2p and O1s XPS spectra of Co3O4/CuO HNCs, respectively.

be attributed to the synergistic effect of the hollow Co3O4and CuO nanostructures [57 -60]. Owing to the particular structures and synergistic effect, oxygen dissolved in solution can adsorb on the Co3O4/ CuO HNC surfaces. The adsorbed molecular oxygen is activated by Co3O4/CuO HNCs to produce1O2and O2 % -, which react with TMB to produce a blue product [67,68].


# 3.3. Optimization of the oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs


To obtain the maximum oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs, the effect of the mass ratio of Cu(NO3)2to ZIF-67on the oxidaselike activity of the obtained Co3O4/CuO HNCs wasfirstly investigated. The results indicate that the oxidase-like activity of the as-prepared Co3O4/CuO HNCs increases with increasing Cu(NO3)2/ZIF-67mass

Fig. 3. (a) UV -Vis absorption spectra with different system in the absence of H2O2. Conditions: 0.10mM TMB, 20mg/L catalyst, pH4.0acetate buffer, 20min and40 °C. (b) Steady-state kinetic assays of Co3O4/CuO HNCs. Conditions: 20mg/L Co3O4/CuO and varied TMB concentrations (0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1, 1.2mM), pH4.0acetate buffer, 20min and40 °C. Error bars denote standard deviations based on three measurements.

ratio from1:10to1:2, above which it decreases (Fig. S6a). This is consistent with a previousfinding that an appropriate precursor ratio plays an important role in generating heterostructured composites [69]. Therefore, we chose the Co3O4/CuO HNCs synthesized with a Cu (NO3)2/ZIF-67mass ratio of1:2for further investigation. Similar to other artificial enzymes, the oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs is also dependent on the solution pH, reaction temperature, and concentration. As shown in Fig. S6b, the catalytic activity of the Co3O4/ CuO HNCs increases with increasing concentration of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs. This can be explained by the fact that the higher the catalyst concentration, the greater the number of catalytic sites [70]. In this work, we used a concentration of20mg/L Co3O4/CuO HNCs. To investigate the effect of the pH and temperature on the oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs, experiments were performed with pH from2to10and temperature from20to80 °C. The optimal pH and reaction temperature are4.0and40 °C (Fig. S6c and S6d), respectively. In summary, the optimal oxidase-like activity of the as-prepared Co3O4/ CuO HNCs is obtained under the following conditions: reaction temperature40 °C, pH4.0, and20mg/L Co3O4/CuO HNCs.


# 3.4. Steady-state kinetics of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs


To better understand the catalytic process, the apparent steady-state kinetic parameters for the reaction between TMB and dissolved oxygen in the presence of Co3O4/CuO HNCs were determined by changing the concentration of TMB and keeping the concentration of Co3O4/CuO HNCs constant at20mg/L. The typical Michaelis -Menten curve is shown in Fig. 3b. The Michaelis -Menten constant ( Km) was obtained by the Lineweaver -Burk equation. The estimated Km value for the Co3O4/ CuO HNCs with TMB as the substrate is0.22mM (Table S2), which is comparable with those for mesoporous silica NPs -Au NPs [18], Hg2+ / citrate -Ag NPs [19], and Co-Fe LDH [71]. However, the Km value for the Co3O4/CuO HNCs with TMB as the substrate is about2to37times lower than that of other nanozymes, including CeO2 [13,67], selenium NPs [72], and lysozyme -Pt NPs [73]. This suggests that the Co3O4/CuO HNCs have a high affinity for TMB.


# 3.5. Detection of DA using the Co3O4/CuO HNCs


As an important catecholamine neurotransmitter, DA plays a crucial role in humans and other mammals [74]. Itsfluctuation in the brain leads to a wide range of diseases and disorders, such as drug addiction, depression, and Parkinson's disease. Therefore, it is essential to develop a simple, sensitive, and selective method for detection of DA in biological samples. In this study, to demonstrate the applicability of the oxidase-like Co3O4/CuO HNCs, a colorimetric assay for DA was developed based on its inhibiting effect on the oxidase-like activity of Co3O4/ CuO HNCs. As shown in Fig. 4a, the absorbance of the TMB -Co3O4/CuO HNC system at652nm gradually decreases with increasing DA concentration from0.05to8 μ M, and a further increase in the DA concentration only causes minor variance of the absorbance at652nm (Fig. 4b). This indicates that the oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs is inhibited in the presence of DA. Fading of the blue color is probably caused by oxidation of DA by dissolved oxygen [75], resulting in consumption of dissolved oxygen andfinally leading to a decrease in production of ROS, such as1O2and O2 % -. This shows the potential of using Co3O4/CuO HNCs for colorimetric assay of DA. The relationship between the inhibited absorbance at652nm ( Δ A652= A0 -A , where A0and A are the absorbance values (at652nm) of the TMB -Co3O4/CuO HNC system in the absence and presence of DA, respectively) and the concentration of DA ( μ M) was evaluated. From the results in Fig. 4b, there is a strong linear relationship between the DA concentration and Δ A652in the range0.05 -8 μ M: Δ A652 =0.0452 [DA] ( μ M)+0.033 ( r2 =0.9940, n =10). The limit of detection (LOD) for DA is0.027 μ M (signal/noise = 3). The present method was compared with other analytical methods for DA analysis in terms of the LOD and linear range

[76 -82]. The linear range of the proposed method for DA is wider than those of other nanozyme-based colorimetric assays (Table1) [77 -79,81,82]. Impressively, the LOD of the proposed method for DA is at least50times lower than that with nanoceria [76], 40times lower than that with Co3O4@NiO core -shell nanotubes [77], 35times lower than that with CuFe2O4/Cu9S8/PPy [78], 20times lower than that with CoFe2O4/CoS [79], 16times lower than that with CuS -rGO [80], 5times lower than that with perovskite LaCoO3NPs [81], and1.9times lower than that with cobalt-doped magnetite/grapheme [82]. This indicates the high sensitivity of Co3O4/CuO HNCs as an oxidase mimic for colorimetric DA determination. The low LOD for DA is attributed to the high oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNCs, which can rapidly catalyze oxidation of TMB to oxTMB in the presence of dissolved oxygen. When DA is introduced into the sensing system, a small amount of DA would consume dissolved oxygen, leading to a decrease in production of ROS, such as1O2and O2 · -. As a result, the solution absorbance at652nm significantly decreases with a perceptible color change from blue to colorless, showing a low LOD for DA.


# 3.6. Selectivity of DA detection


Under above optimized conditions, the selectivity of the Co3O4/CuO HNC nanozyme as a colorimetric probe for DA detection was investigated. The effects of the possible coexisting substances in human serum were evaluated. These include L-histidine (His), L-glycine (Gly), fructose (Fru), lactose, glucose (Glc), Mg2+ , Ca2+ , K + , Na + , leucine, threonine (Thr), L-alanine (Ala), L-tyrosine (Try), and L-arginine (Arg). The experimental results are shown in Fig. 5. The absorbance of the system shows a clear decrease in the presence of10 μ MDA, while there is no significant variation in the absorbance for other substances at the500 μ M level. Hence, the concentrations of the possible interfering substances are in50-fold excess relative to DA and no significant response is observed. In previous studies, the same concentrations of the possible interfering substances and target were used for selectivity evaluation [76 -82]. This suggests that the Co3O4/CuO HNC nanozyme shows good selectivity for detection of DA.


# 3.7. Application of the DA sensor


The practical feasibility of the proposed colorimetric biosensing method was evaluated by analyzing the DA content in human serum samples and DA hydrochloride injection by the standard addition method. Satisfactory recoveries of98.0% to105.0% were obtained for DA in the human serum samples (Table2). The results indicate that the developed method is suitable for detecting the DA level in real samples. Furthermore, the DA content in DA hydrochloride injection was detected by the proposed biosensing method. The results obtained by the proposed method are in good agreement with the stated results (Table S3), further confirming the feasibility and reliability of the proposed method for detection of DA.


# 4. Conclusions


Co3O4/CuO HNC nanocomposites have been successfully prepared and they show enhanced oxidase-like activity compared with pure Co3O4and CuO. The catalytic activity depends on the mass ratio of cobalt to copper, solution pH, temperature, and catalyst concentration. ROS measurements suggest that the nature of the oxidase-like activity of the Co3O4/CuO HNC nanocomposites is probably because of their catalytic ability to activate dissolved O2to generate1O2and O2 % -radicals. Based on the inhibiting effect of DA on TMB oxidation, a new, sensitive, and visual biosensing method has been successfully developed and applied to detection of DA in biological samples. This work provides a simple strategy to fabricate efficient oxidase mimics through MOF-derived hollow nanostructures and it may stimulate interest in exploration of metal oxide nanocomposites as oxidase mimics for
